title: Home
content:
Jesse Straat
Mathematics PhD candidate, Vrije Universiteit Amsterdam

Welcome!

This is the lander of my personal webpage. Here, you can find information about my research, publications, and my LaTeX projects. Moreover, I (try to) keep up with a blog, where I share discussions about niche topics.

About me

I am a mathematics PhD candidate at the Vrije Universiteit Amsterdam, under supervision of Oliver Fabert. I specialise in applying Floer theory methods to quantum mechanics.

title: LaTeX
content: LaTeX This is a collection of projects I have developed in LaTeX. For more information, refer to my CTAN. Packages aeskwadraat CTAN Git This is a package catalogue for my study association A–Eskwadraat. It consists of packages for meeting notes, invoices, beamer presentations, and much more. I largely rewrote a big chunk of the packages, added documentation and even converted parts to LaTeX3. brandstyler CTAN Git This package automates the process of adapting a LaTeX document to an organisation’s official style. Its modular approach allows the user to add their own organisation styles. Contributions of custom organisation styles are welcome. reptheorem CTAN Git When writing a large manuscript, it is sometimes beneficial to repeat a theorem (or lemma or…) at an earlier or later point for didactical purposes. However, thmtools’s built-in restatable only allows replicating theorems after they have been stated, and only in the same document. This package solves the issue by making use of the .aux file, and also introduces its own file extension, .thm, to replicate theorems in other files. collabcomment Git Yet another package for adding author comments for collaborative work (work in progress). I believe it’s more simple than most “competitors”, while not losing any...

title: Research
content:
Research



  
    
      Floer theory and quantum mechanics
      Floer thoery concerns the existence of periodic orbits of (classical) Hamiltonian systems. In particular, it can give a lower bound on the number of periodic orbits, known as cuplength estimates. I aim to expand this formalism to the quantum realm, using new methods and approximations. The ultimate goal is to improve our understanding of quantum mechanics with a dynamical approach.
